hook.question: What do كِتَاب (book), كَاتِب (writer), مَكْتَبَة (library), and كَتَبَ (he wrote) all have in common?
hook.answer: They all come from the same 3 letters: ك - ت - ب. This is the root system — the skeleton key of Arabic.

concept.title: Every Arabic Word Has a 3-Letter Root (Jidhr)
concept.body: Almost every Arabic word is built from a root of exactly 3 consonants. These 3 letters carry a core meaning. Different patterns wrap around that root to create nouns, verbs, and adjectives — all related in meaning. Once you know a root, you can guess the meaning of dozens of words you've never seen.

unlock.insight: Rahman and Raheem in Bismillah are both from root ر-ح-م. But different patterns. Rahman = overwhelmingly, universally merciful (like a downpour). Raheem = continuously, personally merciful (like a flowing stream). Same root. Different patterns. Different shades of mercy. You just understood a theological distinction you've been reciting daily.

quiz.question: How many consonants does a typical Arabic root have?
quiz.explanation: Three consonants form the root. Patterns of vowels and extra letters wrap around them to make different words.

quiz.question: كِتَاب (book) and كَتَبَ (he wrote) share which root?
quiz.explanation: The root is ك-ت-ب, relating to writing. The three letters appear in the same order in every word built from this root.

quiz.question: Rahman and Raheem both come from root ر-ح-م. What does this root relate to?
quiz.explanation: ر-ح-م is the root of mercy. Both names of Allah in Bismillah — Rahman and Raheem — are built from this one root.
